text: Queen's 'Bohemian Rhapsody' has no chorus at all — and it was UK number one twice, in 1975 and again in 1991 after Freddie Mercury died.
The Beatles were originally called The Quarrymen, after John Lennon's school, Quarry Bank High.
U2 formed in 1976 after 14-year-old drummer Larry Mullen Jr. pinned a note to the noticeboard of his Dublin school looking for musicians.
Bono got his nickname from 'Bonavox', a hearing-aid shop on Dublin's North Earl Street.
Thin Lizzy's 'Whiskey in the Jar' is a rocked-up version of a traditional Irish folk song that's centuries old.
Phil Lynott of Thin Lizzy grew up in Crumlin, Dublin — his statue stands just off Grafton Street.
Elvis Presley never performed a single concert outside North America.
ABBA is an acronym of the members' first names: Agnetha, Björn, Benny and Anni-Frid.
Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' is the best-selling studio album of all time.
The blistering guitar solo on Michael Jackson's 'Beat It' was played by Eddie Van Halen — for free.
Dolly Parton reportedly wrote 'Jolene' and 'I Will Always Love You' on the very same day.
The Cranberries' 'Zombie' was written by Dolores O'Riordan about the 1993 IRA bombing in Warrington.
Sinéad O'Connor's 'Nothing Compares 2 U' was written by Prince — his version came first, but hers conquered the world.
Freddie Mercury was born Farrokh Bulsara on the island of Zanzibar.
Freddie Mercury studied graphic design and drew Queen's royal crest himself, working the band members' star signs into it.
Bob Marley's 'No Woman, No Cry' is credited to his friend Vincent Ford, who ran a soup kitchen in Trenchtown — the royalties kept it open.
David Bowie turned down a knighthood in 2003, saying he never intended to accept anything like that.
The Rolling Stones named themselves after a Muddy Waters song, 'Rollin' Stone'.
Westlife have had 14 UK number one singles — more than any act in history except Elvis Presley and The Beatles.
Riverdance began life as a seven-minute interval act at the 1994 Eurovision Song Contest in Dublin.
Ireland and Sweden are tied for the most Eurovision wins ever — seven each.
Ireland's Johnny Logan is the only performer to win Eurovision twice as a singer — and he wrote a third winning song on top of that.
The Beatles' 'Yesterday' had the working title 'Scrambled Eggs' while Paul McCartney hunted for lyrics.
Beyoncé has won more Grammy Awards than any other artist in history.
Daft Punk almost never appeared in public without their robot helmets on.
Adele named her albums after her age when she wrote them: 19, 21, 25 and 30.
Both Gallagher brothers of Oasis are children of Irish emigrants — their mother Peggy is from County Mayo.
Enya is one of Ireland's best-selling artists ever — and she has never toured.
Hozier wrote 'Take Me to Church' in the attic of his parents' house in County Wicklow.
U2's guitarist The Edge was actually born in England, to Welsh parents — his real name is David Howell Evans.
'Fairytale of New York' by The Pogues and Kirsty MacColl famously never reached UK Christmas number one — it was beaten in 1987 by the Pet Shop Boys.
Elton John's real name is Reginald Kenneth Dwight.
Nirvana's 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' got its name from graffiti a friend wrote on Kurt Cobain's wall — he had no idea Teen Spirit was a deodorant brand.
'Gangnam Style' by PSY was the first video ever to hit one billion views on YouTube.
Taylor Swift was named after the singer-songwriter James Taylor.
Ed Sheeran co-wrote Justin Bieber's hit 'Love Yourself' — it started life as an Ed Sheeran song.
AC/DC got their name from the label on a sewing machine belonging to the Young brothers' sister.
Legend has it that when Jimi Hendrix was called the world's greatest guitarist, he replied: 'I don't know — ask Rory Gallagher.'
Queen's 1985 Live Aid set at Wembley is regularly voted the greatest live performance of all time.
Kate Bush's 'Running Up That Hill' hit UK number one in 2022 — 37 years after its release — thanks to Stranger Things.
Wings' 'Mull of Kintyre' was the UK's best-selling single ever until Band Aid's 'Do They Know It's Christmas?' overtook it in 1984.
'Do They Know It's Christmas?' was co-written by a Dubliner — Bob Geldof, with Midge Ure.
Shane MacGowan of The Pogues was born on Christmas Day.
Bruce Springsteen's 'Born in the U.S.A.' is actually a protest song about how Vietnam veterans were treated — not a patriotic anthem.
The Killers' 'Mr. Brightside' is one of the longest-charting songs in UK history — it has spent well over 400 weeks in the Top 100.
Van Morrison's 'Brown Eyed Girl' was originally written as 'Brown Skinned Girl'.
Mozart was composing his own pieces by the age of five.
Madonna is the best-selling female recording artist of all time.
Vinyl records now outsell CDs again — a comeback nobody saw coming in the 2000s.
Fleetwood Mac recorded 'Rumours' while both couples in the band were splitting up — with each other in the room.
Ringo Starr's real name is Richard Starkey.
Madonna's first UK number one single was 'Like a Virgin'.
U2's album 'The Joshua Tree' is named after a tree in Ireland.
ABBA won the Eurovision Song Contest with 'Waterloo'.
Elvis Presley wrote most of his own hit songs.
The Beatles played their famous final rooftop concert in New York.
Bob Dylan has won the Nobel Prize in Literature.
'Dancing Queen' was ABBA's only US number one single.
Thin Lizzy's Phil Lynott was born in Dublin.
The Cranberries came from Cork.
Michael Jackson wrote 'We Are the World' together with Lionel Richie.
'Wonderwall' is on the Oasis album '(What's the Story) Morning Glory?'.
The Spice Girls had five UK Christmas number ones in a row.
Eminem's real name is Marshall Mathers.
'We Will Rock You' and 'We Are the Champions' were released together on the same single.
Riverdance made its debut at a Eurovision held in London.
Johnny Cash recorded a famous live album inside a prison.
'I Will Always Love You' was written by Whitney Houston.
Coldplay's debut album was called 'Parachutes'.
Queen and David Bowie recorded 'Under Pressure' together.
Mick Jagger and Keith Richards first met as adults at art school.
Sinéad O'Connor wrote 'Nothing Compares 2 U' herself.
Van Morrison was the frontman of the band Them before going solo.
Enya was once a member of Clannad.
Westlife are from Dublin.
'Hey Jude' was written by Paul McCartney to comfort John Lennon's son Julian.
Beyoncé started her career in the group Destiny's Child.
Elton John writes the lyrics to his own songs.
Nirvana's Kurt Cobain played guitar left-handed.
Deep Purple's 'Smoke on the Water' is about a fire at a casino in Switzerland.
The Bee Gees were born in Australia.
Amy Winehouse won five Grammy Awards in a single night.
'Despacito' is sung in Portuguese.
Hozier is from County Wicklow.
Sweden has never won the Eurovision Song Contest.
Louis Armstrong recorded 'What a Wonderful World'.
Meat Loaf's 'Bat Out of Hell' is one of the best-selling albums of all time.
Radio stations initially refused to play 'Bohemian Rhapsody' because it was too long.
Prince played nearly all the instruments on his early albums himself.
The Titanic theme 'My Heart Will Go On' was sung by Mariah Carey.
Bono and The Edge wrote the music for a Spider-Man musical on Broadway.
'Sweet Caroline' was written and first recorded by Neil Diamond.
Shakira is from Brazil.
Kylie Minogue starred in 'Home and Away' before her music career.
The Cranberries' 'Zombie' won an MTV Europe Music Award for Best Song.
The Corrs are three sisters and one brother from Dundalk.
Christy Moore was a founding member of Planxty.
'Teenage Dirtbag' was recorded by Blink-182.
'Torn' by Natalie Imbruglia is a cover version.
Britney Spears' '...Baby One More Time' was written by a Swedish songwriter.
'Africa' was recorded by the band Toto.
Robbie Williams left Take That before they ever had a UK number one.
The Cranberries' debut album 'Everybody Else Is Doing It, So Why Can't We?' went to number one in both the UK and Ireland.
Dolores O'Riordan of The Cranberries grew up in County Limerick — she joined the band by answering an ad for a female singer.
Rory Gallagher's battered 1961 Stratocaster is said to have been the first Fender Stratocaster ever brought into Ireland.
Thin Lizzy's 'The Boys Are Back in Town' was nearly left off the Jailbreak album — the band didn't rate it as a single.
Van Morrison's 'Astral Weeks' was recorded in just a handful of sessions in the autumn of 1968.
Snow Patrol's Gary Lightbody is from Bangor, County Down.
The Undertones' 'Teenage Kicks' was John Peel's favourite song of all time — a line from it is carved on his gravestone.
Dana won Ireland's first ever Eurovision in 1970 with 'All Kinds of Everything' — she was just 18.
Niall Horan of One Direction is from Mullingar, County Westmeath.
Sinéad O'Connor refused her Grammy in 1991 — the first artist ever to decline one.
Enya's 'Orinoco Flow' spent three weeks at UK number one in 1988.
Enya lives in a Victorian castle in Killiney, County Dublin, bought with her royalties.
Glen Hansard and Markéta Irglová won an Oscar for 'Falling Slowly' from the Dublin film Once.
Glen Hansard played guitarist Outspan Foster in The Commitments before The Frames took off.
The Script's Danny O'Donoghue was a coach on the very first series of The Voice UK.
B*witched's first four singles all went straight in at UK number one.
Boyzone were put together by Louis Walsh after open auditions in Dublin in 1993.
The Corrs met their manager while auditioning for The Commitments — Andrea landed a small part in the film.
U2 have won more Grammy Awards than any other band in history.
U2's 'One' was written in Berlin during the Achtung Baby sessions that nearly split the band.
The Joshua Tree won U2 the Grammy for Album of the Year in 1988.
In 2014, U2's album Songs of Innocence appeared automatically in half a billion iTunes libraries — to widespread annoyance.
Westlife's cover of 'Uptown Girl' was the official Comic Relief single in 2001.
Ronan Keating co-presented the 1997 Eurovision Song Contest in Dublin.
Ireland hosted Eurovision three years running in the 1990s — 1993, 1994 and 1995.
'Rock 'n' Roll Kids' in 1994 gave Ireland its third Eurovision win in a row.
Chris de Burgh wrote 'The Lady in Red' about his wife Diane.
Gilbert O'Sullivan — born Raymond O'Sullivan in Waterford — spent six weeks at US number one with 'Alone Again (Naturally)'.
'Gloria', one of the most covered garage-rock songs ever, was written by Van Morrison for his Belfast band Them.
Horslips are widely credited with inventing Celtic rock.
The Dubliners appeared on Top of the Pops in 1967 with 'Seven Drunken Nights'.
Fontaines D.C. added the 'D.C.' — for Dublin City — after discovering an American band already called The Fontaines.
Dermot Kennedy busked on Grafton Street for years before selling out arenas.
Imelda May grew up in the Liberties in Dublin.
Ash's members were still doing their A-levels in Downpatrick when their first records charted.
Stiff Little Fingers took their name from a song by The Vibrators.
Two Door Cinema Club formed in Bangor and Donaghadee, County Down.
The Boomtown Rats' 'I Don't Like Mondays' was inspired by a 1979 school shooting in San Diego.
The Boomtown Rats were the first Irish rock band to top the UK singles chart, with 'Rat Trap' in 1978.
Clannad's 'Theme from Harry's Game' made the UK top five — the first Irish-language song ever to do it.
Father Ted's Eurovision spoof 'My Lovely Horse' was co-written by Neil Hannon of The Divine Comedy — he wrote the Father Ted theme too.
The Saw Doctors' 'I Useta Lover' spent nine weeks at Irish number one and was for years the country's best-selling single.
As the story goes, the poet Patrick Kavanagh himself gave 'Raglan Road' to Luke Kelly of The Dubliners in a Dublin pub.
Cork's Sultans of Ping FC had an indie hit about a lost jumper — 'Where's Me Jumper?'
Bell X1 and Damien Rice were once in the same band, Juniper.
The Chieftains were the first Western group to perform on the Great Wall of China, in 1983.
The Coronas' singer Danny O'Reilly is the son of singer Mary Black.
Brian May built his Red Special guitar with his dad, using wood from an old fireplace.
Queen's Brian May has a PhD in astrophysics.
Queen's bassist John Deacon wrote 'Another One Bites the Dust' — and Michael Jackson talked the band into releasing it as a single.
David Bowie retired Ziggy Stardust live on stage at the Hammersmith Odeon in 1973.
The BBC used Bowie's 'Space Oddity' in its coverage of the 1969 moon landing.
A young Elton John played piano on The Hollies' 'He Ain't Heavy, He's My Brother' as a session musician.
Ringo Starr sang lead on 'Yellow Submarine' and 'With a Little Help from My Friends'.
The Beatles hold the record for the most UK number one albums.
George Harrison mortgaged his house to fund Monty Python's Life of Brian — because he wanted to see the film.
The riff for 'Satisfaction' came to Keith Richards in his sleep — he recorded it half-awake and dozed off with the tape running.
Pink Floyd's 'The Dark Side of the Moon' spent more than 900 weeks on the US album chart.
Pink Floyd took their name from two bluesmen: Pink Anderson and Floyd Council.
The Kinks were banned from touring America for four years in the mid-1960s.
Led Zeppelin never released 'Stairway to Heaven' as a single in the UK.
Led Zeppelin's name came from a joke that the band would go down like a lead balloon.
Oasis's 'Wonderwall' never reached UK number one — it was kept off the top by Robson & Jerome.
In the 1995 'Battle of Britpop', Blur's 'Country House' outsold Oasis's 'Roll With It' to number one.
Pulp's Jarvis Cocker invaded the stage during Michael Jackson's performance at the 1996 Brit Awards.
Radiohead released 'In Rainbows' as a pay-what-you-want download.
Chris Martin says the melody of Coldplay's 'Yellow' started as a Neil Young impression.
Adele's '21' is the best-selling album of the 21st century in the UK.
Ed Sheeran played around 300 gigs in 2009 alone, sleeping on friends' sofas between them.
Amy Winehouse wrote 'Rehab' after her family really did try to send her to one.
George Michael said he wrote 'Careless Whisper' at 17, on the bus to his job as a cinema usher.
Wham! were the first Western pop act to play communist China, in 1985.
With 'Wuthering Heights', Kate Bush became the first woman to top the UK chart with a song she wrote herself.
The Sex Pistols' 'God Save the Queen' was kept off UK number one in Jubilee week — amid claims the chart was fixed.
The cover of The Clash's 'London Calling' shows Paul Simonon smashing his bass on stage in New York.
Sting has said himself that 'Every Breath You Take' is a sinister stalker's song, not a love song.
Sting was a schoolteacher before The Police made it.
Mark Knopfler wrote 'Money for Nothing' from things he overheard a deliveryman say in a New York appliance store.
Phil Collins played Live Aid in London AND Philadelphia on the same day — flying between them on Concorde.
Tom Jones's 'It's Not Unusual' was originally meant for Sandie Shaw.
Shirley Bassey has sung three James Bond themes — more than anyone else.
Eurythmics' Annie Lennox and Dave Stewart wrote 'Sweet Dreams' just after splitting up as a couple.
Jim Steinman said 'Total Eclipse of the Heart' began life as a vampire love song.
Slade misspelled their hits on purpose — 'Cum On Feel the Noize', 'Mama Weer All Crazee Now'.
Noddy Holder calls 'Merry Xmas Everybody' his pension — it charts again every single December.
Slade beat Wizzard to the 1973 Christmas number one in the great glam-rock Christmas battle.
Black Sabbath's Tony Iommi lost his fingertips in a factory accident — his workaround invented the downtuned metal sound.
Ozzy Osbourne bit the head off a bat on stage, insisting afterwards he thought it was rubber.
Deep Purple were once listed in the Guinness Book of Records as the world's loudest band.
Iron Maiden's Bruce Dickinson is a qualified airline pilot who has flown the band's own Boeing on tour.
Def Leppard's drummer Rick Allen lost an arm in a car crash and kept the job with a custom electronic kit.
Motörhead's Lemmy was a roadie for Jimi Hendrix.
Elvis Costello was born Declan MacManus — his family roots are Irish.
When Ian Curtis died, Joy Division honoured a pact to retire the name — and became New Order.
New Order's 'Blue Monday' is the best-selling 12-inch single ever — and its fancy die-cut sleeve reportedly lost money on every copy.
The Proclaimers finally got a UK number one in 2007, when '500 Miles' was re-recorded with Peter Kay for Comic Relief.
Every penny of 'Bitter Sweet Symphony' went to the Rolling Stones for 22 years — until Jagger and Richards handed the rights back in 2019.
Arctic Monkeys' debut is the fastest-selling debut album by a band in UK chart history.
Stereophonics took their name from a make of record player.
Girls Aloud were formed on the ITV show Popstars: The Rivals in 2002.
One Direction never actually won The X Factor — they finished third.
Robbie Williams' 'Angels' regularly tops polls of the songs Britons want played at their funerals.
Gary Barlow says he wrote 'Back for Good' in about 15 minutes.
The Spice Girls' 'Wannabe' hit number one in 37 countries.
Geri Halliwell's famous Union Jack dress was a tea towel her sister sewed onto a black dress.
Sam Smith's 'Writing's on the Wall' was the first James Bond theme ever to reach UK number one.
Lewis Capaldi and Doctor Who's Peter Capaldi are related — Peter even starred in the 'Someone You Loved' video.
Fatboy Slim was the bassist in The Housemartins before dance music fame.
Guinness World Records lists Gorillaz as the most successful virtual band ever.
Stormzy funds scholarships for Black British students at Cambridge University.
As the story goes, Florence Welch was signed after singing to a record executive in a nightclub toilet.
'Three Lions' has returned to UK number one in different decades — 1996 and again in 2018.
Band Aid's 'Do They Know It's Christmas?' was written, recorded and mixed inside 24 hours.
An estimated 1.5 billion people watched Live Aid in July 1985.
Lulu won Eurovision for the UK in 1969 — in an unprecedented four-way tie.
Sandie Shaw sang her winning 1967 Eurovision song 'Puppet on a String' barefoot.
Bucks Fizz won Eurovision 1981 with 'Making Your Mind Up' — and that skirt-ripping dance move.
Cliff Richard had UK number one singles in five consecutive decades, from the 1950s to the 1990s.
Mr Blobby beat Take That to the 1993 UK Christmas number one.
LadBaby took five consecutive UK Christmas number ones from 2018 to 2022 — beating The Beatles' record run.
'Bohemian Rhapsody' is the UK's third best-selling single of all time.
Elton John's 'Candle in the Wind 1997' is the UK's best-selling single ever.
The first ever UK singles chart, in 1952, was topped by Al Martino's 'Here in My Heart'.
Top of the Pops ran for 42 years, from 1964 to 2006.
The first Glastonbury was held in 1970, the day after Jimi Hendrix died — tickets cost £1 and included free milk from the farm.
Around 2.5 million people applied for tickets to Oasis at Knebworth in 1996 — roughly 4% of Britain.
Electric Picnic began in 2004 as a one-day boutique festival in Stradbally.
Féile — 'the Trip to Tipp' — packed Semple Stadium in Thurles every summer in the early 1990s.
The first Slane Castle concert in 1981 was headlined by Thin Lizzy — with a young U2 further down the bill.
Freddie Mercury and opera star Montserrat Caballé recorded 'Barcelona' together.
Queen's first UK number one single was 'Bohemian Rhapsody'.
Brian May played guitar on the roof of Buckingham Palace for the Queen's Golden Jubilee.
The Beatles' first single was 'Please Please Me'.
John Lennon's middle name was Winston.
Paul McCartney plays bass left-handed.
The Beatles never won a Grammy.
George Harrison was the youngest Beatle.
'Octopus's Garden' was written by Ringo Starr.
The Rolling Stones have had more UK number one singles than The Beatles.
The Rolling Stones' early hit 'I Wanna Be Your Man' was written by Lennon and McCartney.
David Bowie's real surname was Jones.
David Bowie recorded a Christmas duet with Bing Crosby.
Elton John was once the chairman and owner of Watford Football Club.
Rod Stewart is Welsh.
Tom Jones is from Wales.
Tom Jones's 'Delilah' is a murder ballad.
Dusty Springfield sang 'Son of a Preacher Man'.
Cilla Black was The Beatles' hairdresser before her singing career.
Oasis are from Liverpool.
Blur's Damon Albarn also created Gorillaz.
Pulp are from Sheffield.
Jarvis Cocker wrote 'Common People' about a girl he met at art college.
Radiohead named themselves after a Talking Heads song.
Coldplay were originally called Starfish.
The members of Coldplay met at university in London.
Adele is from Manchester.
Adele's Bond theme 'Skyfall' won an Oscar.
Ed Sheeran was born in Halifax, Yorkshire.
Ed Sheeran appeared in an episode of Game of Thrones.
Amy Winehouse was from Glasgow.
'Valerie' was written by Amy Winehouse.
George Michael and Andrew Ridgeley met at school.
Wham!'s 'Last Christmas' didn't reach UK number one until decades after its release.
Kate Bush wrote 'Wuthering Heights' when she was 18.
Kate Bush is Peter Gabriel's sister.
Peter Gabriel was the original lead singer of Genesis.
Phil Collins joined Genesis as the drummer before becoming its singer.
'In the Air Tonight' was Phil Collins' first solo single.
Sting's real name is Gordon Sumner.
'Message in a Bottle' was The Police's first UK number one.
Mark Knopfler wrote the theme music for the film Local Hero.
'Sultans of Swing' is about a jazz band Mark Knopfler saw playing in a pub.
Queen's drummer Roger Taylor wrote 'Radio Ga Ga'.
Lady Gaga took her stage name from Queen's 'Radio Ga Ga'.
The instrumental 'Albatross' is Fleetwood Mac's only UK number one single.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Dreams' was written by Stevie Nicks.
The Bee Gees wrote 'Islands in the Stream' for Kenny Rogers and Dolly Parton.
Barry and Robin Gibb of the Bee Gees were twins.
Sinéad O'Connor was once ordained as a priest.
In U2's early days, bassist Adam Clayton acted as the band's manager.
Bono's real name is Paul Hewson.
U2's The Edge was born in Wales.
U2 played the Super Bowl half-time show a few months after 9/11.
Twin harmony lead guitars were Thin Lizzy's signature sound.
Phil Lynott published books of poetry.
Thin Lizzy's 'Dancing in the Moonlight' and Toploader's 'Dancing in the Moonlight' are the same song.
'Have I Told You Lately' was written by Van Morrison, not Rod Stewart.
Enya sings mostly in made-up languages.
Enya's siblings were in the band Clannad.
Hozier's 'Take Me to Church' lost the Song of the Year Grammy to Sam Smith's 'Stay with Me'.
Niall Horan is from Cork.
The Script are from Belfast.
Snow Patrol's 'Chasing Cars' was named the most-played song of the 21st century on UK radio.
Westlife recorded a hit duet with Mariah Carey.
Ronan Keating's 'When You Say Nothing at All' was recorded for the film Notting Hill.
Ireland's 1996 Eurovision winner was 'The Voice' by Eimear Quinn.
Riverdance's original lead dancers were Michael Flatley and Jean Butler.
Michael Flatley is from Dublin.
The 'Nothing Compares 2 U' video is almost entirely one close-up of Sinéad O'Connor's face.
The Undertones' 'Teenage Kicks' reached UK number one.
Shane MacGowan was born in Ireland.
Kirsty MacColl's father was the folk singer Ewan MacColl.
Bob Geldof worked as a music journalist in Canada before forming The Boomtown Rats.
Live Aid took place in London and Philadelphia on the same day.
Midge Ure, co-writer of 'Do They Know It's Christmas?', is Scottish.
Ultravox's 'Vienna' was kept off UK number one by Joe Dolce's 'Shaddap You Face'.
ABBA's Björn and Benny wrote the musical Chess with Tim Rice.
ABBA returned in the 2020s with a virtual 'ABBA Voyage' concert show in London.
'Mamma Mia' was ABBA's first UK number one.
The Corrs had a hit with a cover of Fleetwood Mac's 'Dreams'.
A US metal band called Bad Wolves had a huge hit covering The Cranberries' 'Zombie'.
Damien Rice's 'The Blower's Daughter' opens the film Closer.
Glen Hansard and Bono have busked together on Grafton Street on Christmas Eve.
Lisa Hannigan sang with Damien Rice before going solo.
Glen Hansard won his Oscar for the film Sing Street.
Sing Street and The Commitments were made by the same director.
The Commitments is based on a Roddy Doyle novel.
Victoria Beckham had more solo UK number ones than any other Spice Girl.
Girls Aloud's Nadine Coyle is from Derry.
Nadine Coyle was disqualified from Irish Popstars for being under age.
Harry Styles worked in a bakery before The X Factor.
Little Mix were originally called Rhythmix.
Simon Cowell created The X Factor.
Louis Walsh managed both Boyzone and Westlife.
Jedward represented Ireland at Eurovision twice.
Dustin the Turkey once represented Ireland at Eurovision.
Loreen won Eurovision 2012 for Sweden with 'Euphoria'.
The UK has won Eurovision five times.
Bucks Fizz won Eurovision for Ireland.
The Wurzels' 'The Combine Harvester' was a UK number one.
Chas & Dave were from Newcastle.
Madness are from Camden in London.
The Specials' 'Ghost Town' was UK number one during the summer riots of 1981.
UB40 named themselves after an unemployment benefit form.
UB40's 'Red Red Wine' was written by Neil Diamond.
Simply Red's Mick Hucknall started writing 'Holding Back the Years' as a teenager.
Depeche Mode come from Basildon in Essex.
The Cure's Robert Smith also played guitar in Siouxsie and the Banshees.
Duran Duran are named after a character in the film Barbarella.
Boy George's parents were Irish.
The BBC banned Frankie Goes to Hollywood's 'Relax' — which then went to number one.
Rick Astley worked as the studio tea boy before being signed.
Bananarama hold a world record for the most chart entries by an all-female group.
Annie Lennox is Welsh.
Simple Minds are from Edinburgh.
'Don't You (Forget About Me)' was Simple Minds' biggest hit in America.
Franz Ferdinand are named after an archduke.
The Proclaimers are twin brothers.
Biffy Clyro are Scottish.
Lewis Capaldi's 'Someone You Loved' spent seven weeks at UK number one.
Calvin Harris's real name is Adam Wiles.
Snow Patrol formed at university in Scotland.
Two Door Cinema Club are from the Republic of Ireland.
Ash's album '1977' is named after the year the band members were born.
'Molly Malone' was written in the 19th century — by a Scotsman.
The lyrics of 'Danny Boy' were written by an English lawyer.
'The Fields of Athenry' was written in the 1970s.
The Chieftains have won multiple Grammy Awards.
The Dubliners and The Pogues recorded 'The Irish Rover' together.
'Seven Drunken Nights' was banned from Irish radio.
'Riptide' singer Vance Joy is Irish.
Christy Moore's 'Ride On' was written by Christy Moore.
Picture This come from Athy, County Kildare.
Kodaline were previously called 21 Demands.
Freddie Mercury was born in London.
'We Are the Champions' was written by Brian May.
The Beatles came from Manchester.
'Hey Jude' was written by John Lennon.
'Bohemian Rhapsody' was Queen's only UK number one single.
U2 formed in London.
The Cranberries' lead singer was Andrea Corr.
Riverdance made its debut at the Oscars.
Van Morrison is from Dublin.
Thin Lizzy's 'Whiskey in the Jar' is an original song written by Phil Lynott.
'Fairytale of New York' features Cilla Black.
Oasis's 'Wonderwall' was a UK number one single.
Ed Sheeran won The X Factor before his career took off.
Adele names her albums after her lucky numbers.
The Spice Girls had six members.
ABBA are from Norway.
Daft Punk are British.
The Cranberries' 'Zombie' was written about the Vietnam War.
Kate Bush's 'Running Up That Hill' returned to number one thanks to Breaking Bad.
Live Aid took place in 1990.
Bono is U2's drummer.
The Corrs are four sisters.
Johnny Logan won Eurovision for the UK.
'Mr. Brightside' is by Coldplay.
Phil Lynott was Thin Lizzy's drummer.
'Nothing Compares 2 U' was written by Dolly Parton.
The Eurovision Song Contest is held every four years.
Glastonbury festival takes place in Scotland.
